channel.title: The Grant Desk - Research & Journalism, Worldwide, next 90 days
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: Research & Journalism, Worldwide, next 90 days.

item.title: Templeton Foundation: Religion, Science & Society - Intelligence (2026 cycle) - Two tiers: focused studies under USD $260K; larger collaborative initiatives up to USD $2.5M (using subgrants or internal RFP structures). - deadline 15 Jul 2026
item.description:
John Templeton Foundation

Location: Worldwide (Templeton funds charitable organizations inside and outside the US)

Award: Two tiers: focused studies under USD $260K; larger collaborative initiatives up to USD $2.5M (using subgrants or internal RFP structures).

Duration: Three years

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Religion, Science & Society (RSS) funding area, 2026 cycle, Intelligence track. In alignment with the 2026 Foundation-wide Intelligence Venture, RSS welcomes three-year proposals applying theological, philosophical, and social-scientific approaches to the guiding themes outlined in the venture description. Two scales of project are supported: small, focused studies under USD $260K, and larger collaborative initiatives up to USD $2.5M that use subgrants or internal Requests For Proposals to structure the work. RSS prioritises interdisciplinary teams. Proposals may include public-engagement components, but the primary activities must be research. Strong fit for projects bringing humanities, social science, and theology into dialogue with AI/cognitive science framings of intelligence. ELIGIBILITY: The Foundation funds charitable organizations only; per Templeton's Apply for a Grant page, 'individuals or applicants from for-profit companies should not apply' (individuals/for-profits are funded only by rare staff invitation). International organizations are eligible; the Foundation has funded organizations from around the world. US applicants are described publicly only as 'charitable organizations' (formal IRS/501(c)(3) verification is handled inside the Templeton Portal after the Funding Inquiry stage); non-US applicants similarly need to be charitable entities, with equivalency handled through the portal. PROCESS: submit an Online Funding Inquiry (OFI) via the Templeton Portal by 15 July 2026; full proposals are by invitation only after OFI review.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Templeton Foundation: Religion, Science & Society - Meaning Making (2026 cycle) - Only two project sizes accepted in this track: (1) projects between USD $700K and $1.5M (proposals over $1M should use a Requests For Proposals (RFP) structure to support competitively selected subgrants around a shared theme); (2) focused studies under USD $260K investigating a specific dimension of meaning-making. - deadline 15 Jul 2026
item.description:
John Templeton Foundation

Location: Worldwide (Templeton funds charitable organizations inside and outside the US)

Award: Only two project sizes accepted in this track: (1) projects between USD $700K and $1.5M (proposals over $1M should use a Requests For Proposals (RFP) structure to support competitively selected subgrants around a shared theme); (2) focused studies under USD $260K investigating a specific dimension of meaning-making.

Duration: Three years

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Religion, Science & Society (RSS) funding area, 2026 cycle, Meaning Making track. RSS is especially interested in three-year projects that investigate concrete manifestations of meaning making in a world marked by rapid social change, with attention to the practices, experiences, and transitions that enable people to develop, sustain, or renew a life of meaning. STRICT BUDGET WINDOWS: this track will consider ONLY (1) projects between USD $700K and $1.5M (and proposals over $1M must use a Requests For Proposals (RFP) structure with competitively selected subgrants around a shared theme), or (2) focused studies requesting less than USD $260K on a specific dimension of meaning-making. Proposals outside these windows will not be considered. RSS prioritises interdisciplinary teams. Proposals may include public-engagement components, but the primary activities must be research. ELIGIBILITY: The Foundation funds charitable organizations only; per Templeton's Apply for a Grant page, 'individuals or applicants from for-profit companies should not apply' (individuals/for-profits are funded only by rare staff invitation). International organizations are eligible; the Foundation has funded organizations from around the world. US applicants are described publicly only as 'charitable organizations' (formal IRS/501(c)(3) verification is handled inside the Templeton Portal after the Funding Inquiry stage); non-US applicants similarly need to be charitable entities, with equivalency handled through the portal. PROCESS: submit an Online Funding Inquiry (OFI) via the Templeton Portal by 15 July 2026; full proposals are by invitation only after OFI review.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Templeton Foundation: Religion, Science & Society - Imagined Futures (2026 cycle) - Priority on exploratory projects with a request amount up to USD $260K. - deadline 15 Jul 2026
item.description:
John Templeton Foundation

Location: Worldwide (Templeton funds charitable organizations inside and outside the US)

Award: Priority on exploratory projects with a request amount up to USD $260K.

Duration: Three years

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Religion, Science & Society (RSS) funding area, 2026 cycle, Imagined Futures track. RSS is especially interested in three-year projects exploring how religious, philosophical, and narrative traditions (from theology to science fiction) inform a shared sense of imagination and possibility. In this topical area RSS will prioritise exploratory projects with a request amount of up to USD $260K and clear, achievable outputs. Strong fit for speculative, narrative-driven, or futures-oriented research that bridges humanities, religious studies, and science-fiction studies. RSS prioritises interdisciplinary teams. Proposals may include public-engagement components, but the primary activities must be research. ELIGIBILITY: The Foundation funds charitable organizations only; per Templeton's Apply for a Grant page, 'individuals or applicants from for-profit companies should not apply' (individuals/for-profits are funded only by rare staff invitation). International organizations are eligible; the Foundation has funded organizations from around the world. US applicants are described publicly only as 'charitable organizations' (formal IRS/501(c)(3) verification is handled inside the Templeton Portal after the Funding Inquiry stage); non-US applicants similarly need to be charitable entities, with equivalency handled through the portal. PROCESS: submit an Online Funding Inquiry (OFI) via the Templeton Portal by 15 July 2026; full proposals are by invitation only after OFI review.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: EHRI Conny Kristel Fellowship Programme 2026 - Stipend for housing and living expenses + travel reimbursement (amount varies by host country) - deadline 07 Jun 2026
item.description:
EHRI-ERIC (European Holocaust Research Infrastructure)

Location: Across 25 host institutions in Europe, Israel and the United States

Award: Stipend for housing and living expenses + travel reimbursement (amount varies by host country)

Duration: 1 to 6 weeks per fellowship

Deadline: 07 Jun 2026

Fellowship programme supporting Holocaust research by giving researchers, archivists, librarians, curators and other professionals access to 25 key archives and research institutions across Europe, Israel and the USA. Fellows design their own research journey of 1 to 6 weeks and receive a stipend plus travel reimbursement. Welcomes projects on all aspects of Holocaust history (prehistory, legacy, archival management) and is particularly open to PhD students and early-career practitioners.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: National Geographic x The Climate Pledge: Illuminating Climate Solutions RFP 2026 - Up to $100,000 per project (recommended cap of $20,000 if you have <=5 years of experience). Funds for project costs only, used over up to 2 years. - deadline 25 May 2026
item.description:
National Geographic Society (in partnership with The Climate Pledge)

Location: Worldwide (storytellers globally; preference for those with lived experience or established local collaborations in the communities they cover)

Award: Up to $100,000 per project (recommended cap of $20,000 if you have <=5 years of experience). Funds for project costs only, used over up to 2 years.

Duration: Up to 24 months. First-time recipients also join the National Geographic Explorer Community (training courses, software tools, future funding eligibility).

Deadline: 25 May 2026

Open RFP for storytellers producing solutions-grounded climate journalism and media projects. National Geographic Society and The Climate Pledge are funding a global cohort of Explorers to build a portfolio of stories on climate resilience and solutions, with the explicit goal of moving business leaders and policymakers to act. Story themes (non-exhaustive): Climate & Energy Transition (carbon-free energy, decarbonization); Nature & Land Systems (water stewardship, nature-based solutions, biodiversity, restoration, adaptation, regenerative agriculture, food systems); Built & Human Systems (built environment, climate migration, public health, social adaptation); Adaptation in Extreme Weather (hurricanes, heat waves, wildfires, floods). All story ideas must be grounded in solutions. Eligibility: open to both existing NatGeo Explorers and applicants new to the program. Applicants must show a record of successful media projects and submit a portfolio (e.g. website). Storytellers with lived experience in (or established collaborative relationships with) the communities they cover are prioritised. Up to $100,000 per project (recommend up to $20,000 if 5 or fewer years of experience). Budgets must consist of reasonable, well-justified costs directly required to complete the project, used over up to 2 years. All applications must include an explicit plan for evaluating impact. Submit only via the NatGeo online portal in English (the individual responsible for the project must be the listed project leader; one proposal per applicant as project lead). Questions: funding@ngs.org with subject 'RFP Illuminating Climate Solutions'. Deadline: 25 May 2026, 11:59 PM US-EDT.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).
